My predecessor, Silas, wrote that writing is the only way to remain sane. I am not certain sanity is still within my reach.

The Instant Legion had never been touched by any attack in the history of their existence. They were not merely fast. Speed, as a concept, implies a measurable velocity, a distance covered over a duration of time. The Instant Legion existed in the spaces between Planck seconds, moving through a hyper-temporal dimension where the very notion of duration was optional. They could strike an infinite number of times before their target's nervous system registered the first blow. They could kill gods in the gap between a thought and its execution. They had never lost a battle because battles require time, and time was a medium they had transcended. They came to Thronegate in numbers beyond counting, an endless legion of hyper-velocity warriors who believed that even Godric, with all his supposed omniscience and omnipotence, could not react to an attack that occurred before reaction was physically possible.

Godric was in the throne room, reviewing agricultural reports from the outer provinces. He had a particular fondness for agricultural reports. They reminded him of Olympus, he once told me, of the simple rhythms of mortal life before the fall. I was standing at his side, my amplified perception struggling to track the faintest shimmer of the Instant Legion's approach. They were everywhere and nowhere, surrounding the throne room in every fraction of every moment, preparing to unleash a simultaneous strike across all temporal coordinates. The attack, when it came, would land in the past, present, and future at the exact same instant, an assault that no being, no matter how powerful, could possibly evade or survive.

Godric set down his agricultural report. He looked at me with a slight, thoughtful frown, and he said that he had been curious about his speed. He wondered how fast he could move if he shifted his weight from one foot to the other. A casual, absent-minded gesture. The sort of movement a man might make while standing in line at a market or waiting for a pot of tea to steep. He stood up from the throne—not quickly, not urgently, but with the relaxed, unhurried motion of a man rising to stretch his legs—and he shifted his weight from his left foot to his right.

The movement was infinitesimal. To my amplified eyes, it was simply a man adjusting his stance. But Godric's speed stat had been multiplying at rates beyond infinite recursion every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for over ninety-one thousand years. The casual weight shift that I perceived as a simple, mundane gesture was, in actuality, the fastest motion that had ever occurred in the history of existence. Godric's body traversed the microscopic distance of that weight shift at a velocity that transcended the concept of velocity. The friction of his movement through the fabric of reality generated heat beyond the Planck temperature. The kinetic displacement shattered the dimensional barriers between every timeline, every reality, every plane of existence simultaneously.

The Instant Legion, attacking from every moment at once, found themselves intercepted not by a counterattack but by the simple, catastrophic fact of Godric's movement. His weight shift occurred in all moments. It occurred in the past before their assault had been conceived. It occurred in the present where their blades were descending. It occurred in the future where they had already achieved victory in their own temporal calculations. The speed differential was so vast that the Legion's fastest warriors, moving at their absolute maximum velocity through the gaps between Planck seconds, appeared completely frozen compared to Godric's casual weight shift. They did not have time to register his movement. They did not have time to perceive their own destruction. They were simply erased from every moment they occupied, their hyper-temporal forms shredded into non-existence by the sheer, passive force of Godric's speed displacing the medium through which they moved.

The omniverse was annihilated by the kinetic backlash. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension was torn apart by the speed differential. I existed in the void of nothingness, preserved by Godric's protective auras, and I screamed without sound for what felt like an eternity. When the omniverse was restored—in zero seconds, as it always is—the Instant Legion was gone. They had never been touched by any attack in the history of their existence until they attacked Godric, and then they were touched by nothing more than the King shifting his weight from one foot to the other.

Godric completed his weight shift and stood comfortably, his posture relaxed, his expression mildly curious. He looked at the empty air where the Instant Legion had been and nodded slowly, as if confirming a private hypothesis. Then he turned to me, his salt-and-pepper beard framing a warm, concerned smile, and he asked if I was feeling well. He said I looked pale. He suggested I visit the palace physician for a calming draught. He then returned to his agricultural reports, and I came here, and I am writing this, and I do not know if I will ever stop shaking.

---

Kaedan closed Petrus's journal with hands that were no longer trembling. The trembling had been replaced by a profound, almost meditative stillness. It was the stillness of a mind that had processed too much horror to respond with simple physiological fear. Four journals. Four court wizards. Four incidents spanning tens of thousands of years. The Granite Emperor destroyed by the flex of a single muscle fiber. The Silent Hunters destroyed by the act of listening. The Eternal Maw destroyed by the act of waiting. The Instant Legion destroyed by a shift of weight from one foot to the other. In every case, Godric had exerted effectively zero percent of his true power. In every case, the omniverse had been annihilated. In every case, Godric had restored everything in zero seconds and then expressed warm, paternal concern for his court wizard's wellbeing. The pattern was so consistent, so utterly unwavering, that Kaedan felt a strange, hollow calm settling over him. He understood now. He understood why his father had looked at him with pity. He understood why every court wizard before him had written their testimony. The journals were not tactical analyses. They were not arcane observations. They were confessions. Confessions of men who had stared into an abyss so vast that the abyss itself was an understatement, and who had chosen, despite everything, to continue serving the being at its center.

He reached for the final journal.

---

**Year 412,078 — Merek — Luck**

My name is Merek. I am thirty-five years old. I have been Godric's court wizard for fifteen years, and I am writing this account with a bottle of aged brandy at my elbow because sobriety is no longer a useful state of consciousness for processing the events I have witnessed. If my handwriting is unsteady, you will understand why.

The Probability Throne was not a single entity but a collective civilization of beings who had achieved absolute, mathematically perfect mastery over causality and chance. They were not conquerors in the conventional sense. They did not wield weapons or armies. They wielded probability itself. They could calculate the outcome of any event with infinite precision, and they could manipulate those outcomes to ensure their victory before any conflict began. They had never lost. The concept of losing had been removed from their reality. They had reshaped entire multiverses by simply adjusting the probability fields of their civilizations, causing their enemies' weapons to malfunction, their stars to go nova at precisely the wrong moment, their alliances to collapse under absurd chains of coincidence and misfortune. They came to Thronegate not with a threat but with a challenge. They proposed a game—a cosmic wager in which the fate of the omniverse would be decided by the purest expression of chance. They believed that even Godric, with all his physical might and arcane supremacy, could not control something as fundamentally chaotic as luck. They believed they had found the one domain where their mastery exceeded his.

Godric accepted the challenge with a warm, almost avuncular smile. He was in the grand reception hall, receiving the Probability Throne's delegation with the same relaxed, friendly demeanor he extended to all guests. He listened to their terms, nodded along as they explained the intricate rules of their cosmic wager, and then remarked, almost to himself, that he had never properly tested his luck stat. He wondered how it worked. He did not understand the mechanism, he admitted, because luck was not something one could consciously control. It was a passive attribute. He was curious to see what would happen.

The wager began. The Probability Throne had stacked every possible variable in their favor. They had calculated trillions of trillions of branching timelines and adjusted the probability fields of the entire omniverse to ensure their victory. By every rational metric, their success was a mathematical certainty. They activated their probability engines, vast machines that hummed with the accumulated causal manipulation of a billion years of civilization, and they waited for the dice of reality to fall in their favor.

The dice did not fall. The dice exploded. The Probability Throne's probability engines, designed to manipulate chance across infinite realities, encountered Godric's luck stat and immediately suffered catastrophic, paradoxical system failures. Godric's luck had been multiplying at rates beyond infinite recursion every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for over four hundred thousand years. It was not a conscious ability. It was not a power he could activate or deactivate. It was a fundamental, passive law of his existence, as intrinsic to his being as the blood in his veins. And when the Probability Throne attempted to manipulate probability against him, his luck responded with the violent, disproportionate force of an immune system attacking a pathogen.

The Probability Throne's machines reversed their own causality. Their carefully calculated favorable outcomes inverted into the most catastrophically unfavorable outcomes possible. The energy reserves of their civilization, intended to power their probability engines for eternity, spontaneously converted into anti-probability energy that began erasing their own existence from the timeline. Every weapon they had ever built malfunctioned simultaneously, detonating within their own cities. Every defensive system they had ever erected collapsed, crushing their populations beneath the rubble of their own hubris. Every starship in their fleet suffered simultaneous, impossible engine failures and plummeted into their own suns. The chain of absurd, catastrophic coincidences cascaded across their entire civilization, each event more improbable than the last, each disaster feeding into the next with the cruel, methodical precision of a cosmic joke.

Godric did not move. He did not raise a hand. He did not channel a single ounce of magical energy. He simply stood in the grand reception hall, watching with polite curiosity as the Probability Throne's delegation was consumed by the backlash of their own rigged game. Their leader, a being of pure mathematical consciousness, screamed as its probability matrix collapsed inward, crushing its abstract existence into a singularity of absolute, impossible misfortune. The entire civilization of the Probability Throne was erased from the omniverse in a cascade of statistically impossible disasters that lasted less than a single second and yet felt, to those experiencing it, like an eternity of personalized, merciless torment.

The omniverse was not annihilated by collateral damage on this occasion, though the local dimensional fabric suffered severe probability burns that Godric later restored with a casual thought. The destruction was more targeted, more precise, as if Godric's luck had calculated exactly how much force was needed to destroy the opposition without inconveniencing its master more than necessary. The Probability Throne was simply gone, their trillions of years of civilization reduced to a faint, lingering echo of statistical improbability that would haunt the local multiverse for the next several millennia.

Godric turned to me with a slight, puzzled expression. His salt-and-pepper beard caught the warm light of the reception hall's chandeliers. His ruggedly handsome face was creased with the kind of mild confusion a man might display upon discovering an unexpected charge on his account ledger. "Merek," he said slowly, "I am not entirely certain what just happened. Did my luck do that? I was not even trying to use it." He paused, stroking his beard thoughtfully. "How remarkably convenient. I suppose that is rather the point of luck, is it not?"

I did not know how to respond. I still do not know how to respond. I am his loyal court wizard. I love him as my bloodline has always loved him. But I have just watched an entire civilization of probability-masters be destroyed by a force that Godric did not even know he was using, a force that acted entirely on its own, a force so absurdly, impossibly powerful that the concept of chance itself has become a servant to his existence. He did not destroy the Probability Throne. His passive luck did, while he was standing there trying to figure out what was happening. The gap between Godric and the rest of the omniverse is not a gap. It is an infinite, unbridgeable chasm, and even the parts of him that he does not consciously control are more than sufficient to annihilate anything that opposes him.

I have finished my brandy. I am going to drink another bottle. If you are reading this, future court wizard, I am sorry. I am so deeply, profoundly sorry.

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. The quiet of the Scriptorium was absolute, broken only by the faint, distant hum of the palace's eternal enchantments and the soft crackle of the arcane lamps. He sat perfectly still for a long moment, his hands resting flat on the worn leather cover of Merek's journal, his breath slow and measured. The cold pressure that had been building in his spine since the first journal had reached its culmination. It was no longer a pressure. It was a solid, permanent weight, a new organ of dread that had grafted itself onto his consciousness and would never be removed.

Five journals. Five court wizards. Five incidents. Strength. Senses. Endurance. Speed. Luck. In every case, Godric had exerted effectively zero percent of his true power. In every case, the opposition had been annihilated with such overwhelming, effortless finality that the very concept of resistance had been rendered absurd. In every case, Godric had then expressed warm, genuine, paternal concern for the wellbeing of the traumatized wizard standing beside him. He did not understand the horror he inflicted. He could not understand. The gap between his infinite scaling and the finite minds of his court wizards was itself infinite, and bridging that gap was a mathematical impossibility.

Kaedan understood now. Opposing Godric was not dangerous. It was not foolish. It was not even impossible. It was a category error, a fundamental misunderstanding of the structure of reality. You could no more oppose Godric than a drop of water could oppose the ocean. The ocean would not even notice the drop's defiance. It would simply absorb it, or evaporate it, or ignore it entirely, and the drop would cease to have ever existed as a separate entity. The Granite Emperor, the Silent Hunters, the Eternal Maw, the Instant Legion, the Probability Throne—these had been the mightiest drops in the omniverse, beings of staggering, cosmic power that had challenged pantheons and conquered realities. And Godric had destroyed them all, with the flex of a muscle fiber, with the tilt of his head, with the act of waiting, with a shift of weight, with a luck stat he did not even know he was using.

The door to the Scriptorium opened. Aldric stepped inside, his ancient eyes immediately finding Kaedan's face, reading the stillness there, the new weight. He did not ask if Kaedan had finished. He simply nodded, and there was a depth of understanding in that nod that made Kaedan's chest ache.

"You see it now," Aldric said quietly. It was not a question.

"I see it," Kaedan replied. His voice was steady. Steadier than he had expected. "Victory against him was impossible before opposition began. Not just in practice. In principle. The mathematics of his existence do not permit the concept of challenge to even exist. Everything that has ever opposed him was already defeated before it formed its first thought of defiance."

Aldric moved to the table and placed his weathered hand on the stack of journals. "These records exist not to warn us of danger," he said. "There is no danger. Godric's enemies are less than dust to him. These records exist to warn us of the psychological weight of serving a being whose power cannot be measured, whose mercy is as infinite as his cruelty, and whose kindness to us is genuine even as his destruction of others is absolute. The dissonance is the burden we carry. We are the companions of an apex predator who loves us like family. We must hold both truths in our minds simultaneously, or we will break."

Kaedan looked at his father, at the deep lines carved into his face by decades of carrying that very dissonance. Aldric had served Godric for fifty-four years. He had witnessed horrors that his own journals, somewhere in this vast archive, recorded in detail. And he was still here, still loyal, still loving the King who had destroyed the Granite Emperor with a flexed finger and then asked if Duran wanted honey pastries.

"Father," Kaedan said, "does it ever become easier?"

Aldric was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was the soft, tired rasp of a man who had long ago made peace with unanswerable questions. "No," he said. "It does not become easier. But it becomes... familiar. You learn to hold the dread and the love together. You learn to serve the King because he is worth serving, despite everything he is capable of, perhaps because of everything he is capable of. He is the warmest, kindest, most genuinely loving tyrant the omniverse will ever know. And he needs us—not for protection, not for guidance, not for magic—but for companionship. He needs someone to sit with him in the garden and listen to him read poetry. He needs someone to share honey pastries with. He needs someone who will look at him and see not the apex predator but the mortal king of Olympus, the father who lost his daughters, the man who has been alone for ten thousand centuries."

Kaedan felt the weight in his spine shift slightly, not diminishing but settling into a more comfortable position. He would learn to carry it. Every court wizard before him had learned. Every court wizard after him would learn. The journals would be here, waiting, their voices speaking across the millennia to each new generation, bearing witness to the impossible, terrifying, absurdly overwhelming truth of Godric's power.

He stood from the table, his legs steady beneath him, and looked at the towering shelves of journals that surrounded him. Thousands upon thousands of volumes. Thousands upon thousands of court wizards. Each one had sat where he was sitting now. Each one had felt what he was feeling now. Each one had chosen to serve anyway.

"I am ready," Kaedan said. "I would like to see him now."

Aldric smiled, a small, sad, proud smile, and led his son out of the Scriptorium. The journals remained behind, silent and patient, waiting for the next terrified young wizard to open their covers and learn, as Kaedan had learned, that Godric did not merely win. Victory against him had been impossible before the first atom of the first opposition had coalesced in the void. And yet, in the gardens of the Eternal Palace, a ruggedly handsome forty-five-year-old man with a salt-and-pepper beard was waiting with a pot of jasmine tea and a warm, genuine smile, ready to welcome his newest court wizard into the family. The horror and the love existed together, inseparable and eternal, the twin foundations of a dynasty that had ruled the omniverse for a million years and would rule it forever.

content.text:
Use "Godric Stat Experiment" prompt as baseline writing rule for the journal.

Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal. Choose superpowers that are distinctly different from each other). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more highly overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger (You must note that this increase in power if applied to any powerful entity in the omniverse will be considered to be absurdly high, but for Godric whose base power is already so astronomically high, even if he absorbs the power of the entire omniverse, it will increase his power by amount so infinitesimal, pretty much 0, relative to his base power). Even with its base effect, nerfed to 0% of its power, each superpower is enough to completely destroy the omniverse infinite times. You must ensure that the display of this superpower is even more absurdly destructive than the Godric's journals about his stats (highly exaggerate, when you think it’s already exaggerated, exaggerate more and more), to the point of causing immediate existential dread upon reading it. Avoid philosophical, abstract concepts & powers, or metaphysical narrative--they will just obscure the narrative and make the intended impact less.

Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
Okay, the user is continuing the "Godric Stat Experiment" narrative. They've provided the master lore and the previous context, including the framing story where Kaedan reads journals about Godric's stat tests. Now they want me to write the next part, focusing on superpower journals. The prompt is very specific.

I need to parse all the instructions. The core is to write three detailed journal excerpts, each by a different court wizard, each witnessing Godric "testing" a single superpower. The superpowers must be distinct. The tone and scale must be even more absurd and destructive than the stat journals, to the point of causing immediate existential dread upon reading. The framing POV is still Kaedan reading these.

Let me break down the requirements. I need to use the "Godric Stat Experiment" prompt as the baseline, which I already have in the history. That means maintaining the prose-only format, the exaggerated tone, the specific keywords (absurdity, exponential scaling, existential dread, etc.), and all the rules about Godric's effortlessness, the opposition's futility, the collateral damage scale, and the court wizard dynamics.

The user emphasizes the evolved weakness paradox. So each superpower must have multiple enhanced effects, some making Godric permanently stronger, but this increase is infinitesimally small relative to his base power. The base effect, nerfed to 0%, is enough to destroy the omniverse infinite times. The display must be more absurd than the stat journals. I need to exaggerate heavily.

I must avoid philosophical, abstract concepts or powers. So superpowers like "Concept Erasure" or "Narrative Control" might be too abstract. I need something more concrete but still cosmically destructive. Let's think of distinct superpowers: maybe "Absolute Annihilation" which is about pure destructive force, "Absolute Transmutation" which changes things, and "Absolute Projection" which is about spawning other supreme beings. Those are different categories: destruction, alteration, and creation/control.

Each journal needs a distinct court wizard with a unique voice. I can give them different ages, backgrounds, and ways of processing trauma. One could be young and idealistic, another older and more philosophical, another analytical. Their reactions should vary but all end in profound dread.

The framing is Kaedan's POV. He's just finished reading the stat journals. Now he's reading these superpower journals. I need to show his progression, his dread deepening. The father, Aldric, is likely still there. The user mentions the father's role earlier. I can have an interaction between them before and after.

The user says "Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals." So the story is Kaedan's experience reading them, not just the journals themselves. I need to balance the journal excerpts with Kaedan's reactions and the framing narrative.

Let's structure it. Start with Kaedan still in the Scriptorium or maybe called back. His father tells him there are more journals—these are about superpowers, which are even worse. Kaedan reads them one by one, with his reactions getting more intense. After the last one, a final realization or conversation with his father.

Now, for the superpowers. The first: "Absolute Annihilation." It's simple destructive force, but evolved to an absurd degree. The opposition could be a being that thought it could survive anything. The enhanced effects could be things like spatial disregard (annihilating from everywhere at once), temporal cancellation (destroying them in past, present, future), targeting their conceptual existence, and the destruction keeps going recursively. The court wizard could be Valen, young, optimistic, completely shattered by witnessing pure, overwhelming destruction.

The second: "Absolute Transmutation." This is about changing matter/energy/reality. The opposition could be something like an Ever-Adapting entity that thought it could adapt to anything. The enhanced effects could be overriding all resistances, violating conservation laws, telekinetic/anatomic manipulation, permanent adaptive programming (the victims keep transmuting forever), and the power creating transmutation points that seek out targets. The court wizard could be Orin, older, more philosophical, whose understanding of reality's stability is destroyed.

The third: "Absolute Projection." This is about Godric manifesting his power externally, like creating autonomous, sentient projections. The opposition could be an Infinite Legion, endless warriors. The enhanced effects could be the projections being completely autonomous, each more powerful than the entire omniverse, capable of infinite duplication, and capable of self-evolution in combat. The horror is that Godric doesn't even need to act; his projections handle everything while he watches. The court wizard could be Felix, an analyst, whose logical understanding of force projection is shattered.

Each journal must emphasize the absurd scale. When describing destruction, I need to go beyond the stat journals. The stat journal about strength destroyed the omniverse with a flex of a muscle fiber. So a superpower must be even more catastrophic. Maybe the Annihilation power doesn't just destroy the omniverse; it erases the memory of the target from existence, or the destruction cascades into higher dimensions. The Transmutation power doesn't just change the target; it changes the fundamental laws of the local reality as a side effect. The Projection power doesn't just create minions; each minion is a self-contained apocalypse that makes the original opposition look like nothing.

For the dread, I need to make the court wizards' reactions visceral. Physical symptoms, mental fractures, the sense of their entire worldview collapsing. The journals should be confessions of their shattered understanding.

The user also mentions the evolved weakness paradox has made Godric permanently stronger from these events, but the increase is "infinitesimally 0" relative to his base. I should note that in each journal—like, after the annihilation, Godric's power grows slightly, but even this "slight" growth is more than the combined power of the omniverse, yet to Godric it's nothing. That adds to the absurdity.

I must avoid "abstract, philosophical concepts" so the powers should have concrete, observable effects. Annihilation turns things to nothing with excessive violence. Transmutation changes things in grotesque, physical ways. Projection creates tangible, overpowered entities.

Let's outline the story flow. Start with Kaedan's perspective. He thinks he's done after the stat journals, but his father brings out the superpower journals. He's already shaken, and his father warns him these are worse. The father's demeanor should show his own trauma from these records.

Journal 1: Valen on Absolute Annihilation. Describe the opposition (The Eternal Fortress, maybe). Godric tests the superpower on a whim. He uses it, and the destruction is so absolute that even the narrator can't fully perceive it. The opposition is erased with such violence that reality itself hemorrhages. Collateral damage is omniversal annihilation, restored. Godric's casual reaction. Valen's breakdown.

Transition: Kaedan's hands shaking, but he continues.

Journal 2: Orin on Absolute Transmutation. Opposition: The Adaptive Swarm. Godric transmutes them. The transmutation isn't just a change; it's an infection that rewrites their very nature, turning their adaptation against them. The swarm is turned into a monument of screaming flesh, their adaptive ability now a curse. The power even changes the concept of matter in the area. Orin's philosophical horror.

Transition: Kaedan feeling nauseous, realizing the pattern, the obscene creativity of destruction.

Journal 3: Felix on Absolute Projection. Opposition: The Infinite Legion. Godric creates projections. Each projection is a god of destruction. The Legion is slaughtered, but the horror is the projections' autonomy—they're infinitely powerful, ruthlessly loyal, and Godric could create infinite more. Felix realizes Thronegate's military might is irrelevant; Godric's mere projections could solo all of existence. The projections are so powerful they could erase the omniverse if not controlled. Felix's analytical dread.

Ending: Kaedan finishes. He's beyond trembling, in a state of profound shock. His father enters. They have a brief conversation. Kaedan verbalizes his new understanding: Godric's stats were measurement, but the superpowers are a whole different category of creative, overwhelming, personal annihilation. He realizes that even the most apathetic flicker of Godric's curiosity could drown the omniverse in novas of unprecedented agony. The weight settles. He's ready to serve, with this new, deeper dread. The final line should resonate, like "the silence stretched ahead of him, a million years of service waiting to be filled with loyalty and love and the endless, quiet echo of the journals' warnings."

I need to write in prose only, no bullet points in the final response. The journal excerpts should be clearly separated with headings like the stat journals were (Year, Name, Superpower). The language should be rich, sensory, exaggerated. I'll use strong imagery for the destruction and the court wizards' trauma.

Let me check the master lore for superpower rules. "Beginning at the Stasis Event, Godric gains one new apocalyptic superpower every second forever. Each power, at the instant it appears, is already capable of causing instant extinction to an entire civilization. The potency of every superpower also multiplies by 1,000 and by itself every Planck second. Later, because of the Weakness Paradox, this original 1,000-times framework becomes outdated. The rate becomes far beyond ordinary infinity... Even the base effect of a superpower, when nerfed to 0% of its power, is enough to destroy the entire singular omniverse infinite times over. Superpower displays should feel even more absurdly destructive than Godric’s amplified stat." This aligns with the prompt.

Also, "Around Year 2000, the weakness paradox had already collected every possible immunity and mastery... The omniverse overcompensates... enhances his amplification rate... adds increasingly devastating effects to his powers..." So by the time of these journals (years are huge, like 600,000+), the superpowers have evolved multiple times with multiple enhanced effects. I need to explicitly mention some of these effects per journal, as the prompt asks.

For each superpower, I'll conceive an enhanced effects list. For "Absolute Annihilation": maybe it doesn't need a target, it simply removes a region of spacetime; it can chain-react to annihilate connected entities; the act of annihilation generates a surge that makes Godric permanently slightly stronger (but the increase is nothing to him). For "Absolute Transmutation": it can bypass all resistances; it can transmute non-material concepts like souls or magic; it can make the transmutation source-perpetual (self-sustaining); the transmutation can spread like a plague. For "Absolute Projection": projections are sentient, omniscient, omnipotent relative to creation; they can duplicate infinitely; they learn and evolve; they're fiercely loyal; one projection is enough to dismantle the omniverse; Godric could spawn infinite of them.

I'll weave these into the narrative.

The court wizards' voices: Valen (idealistic, young, poetic in his horror). Orin (methodical, tries to analyze, but is defeated). Felix (analytical, focuses on the strategic implications, his dread is colder, more logical). Their ages can vary. Valen 33, Orin 48, Felix late 50s. Their journals should reflect their personality.

Now, the dread upon reading. Kaedan's reaction to each should escalate. After the stat journals, he was shaken but functional. After the first superpower journal, he might feel physically ill. After the second, his understanding of reality might crack further. After the third, a profound, silent terror. His conversation with his father at the end should show he's now truly initiated into the deepest level of the court wizard's burden.

I must ensure no "show, don't tell" restriction is violated—the prompt says I can state plainly absurd power levels. I'll do that. "His power was not a force; it was an anti-force that consumed force itself." "The entire omniverse was erased a googolplexian times over." Etc.

Let's start writing. The response will be the full narrative.
messages.content:
The weight of the five stat journals had not yet settled into Kaedan's bones when his father returned to the Scriptorium carrying a second, smaller stack of volumes. These books were different. Their leather bindings were charred at the edges, warped by proximity to arcane forces that had overflowed their protective wards. The air around them felt thicker, heavier, as though the words inside had saturated the very paper with something approaching radiation. Aldric set them on the obsidian table with a care that bordered on reverence, his weathered hands lingering on the topmost volume for a moment before he withdrew them.

"The stat journals are terrible," Aldric said quietly. "But they are, at their core, records of measurement. Strength multiplied beyond infinity. Speed multiplied beyond infinity. These are comprehensible horrors. You can look at a number, even an impossible number, and understand that it is large." He paused, and Kaedan saw his father's jaw tighten almost imperceptibly. "Superpowers are different. Each one is a unique apocalypse. Each one has evolved, through the repeated triggering of the Evolved Weakness Paradox, to possess multiple enhanced effects that go far beyond the original function. The base effect alone, nerfed to zero percent of its power, can destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over. But the enhanced effects..." Aldric shook his head slowly. "The enhanced effects are the reason these journals are kept separate. Read them. And remember that Godric does not understand why we find them disturbing. He thinks we overreact."

He left without another word, and Kaedan sat in the suffocating silence, staring at the singed leather of the first volume. His magically amplified senses could detect the faint psychic residue left by its author, a lingering scream of existential horror pressed between the pages like a fossilized shadow. He had thought the stat journals had prepared him. He was wrong.

He opened the first volume.

---

**Year 613,442 — Valen — Superpower: Absolute Annihilation**

I am Valen, thirty-three years of age, and I have been Godric's court wizard for thirteen years. Before I witnessed what I am about to record, I believed I understood the nature of destruction. I had read the stat journals. I had studied the accounts of my predecessors with the solemn diligence expected of my bloodline. I knew that Godric could destroy the omniverse with the flex of a muscle fiber. I knew that his speed could shatter reality with a shift of weight. I knew these things intellectually, the way a child knows that the sun is hot without ever having touched its surface. Intellectual knowledge and witnessed experience are separated by an infinite chasm, and I have now crossed that chasm. I do not expect to ever fully return.

The entity called itself the Unraveler. It was not a conqueror or a destroyer in the conventional sense. It was a sentient negation, a living absence that had existed before the first universe coalesced from the primordial void. Its purpose was not to kill or to rule but to simplify—to reduce the chaotic multiplicity of existence back to the clean, quiet emptiness from which all things emerged. It had unmade entire multiverses not through force but through ontological reversal, gently, patiently persuading reality to forget itself. It did not hate existence. It pitied existence. It believed that the omniverse was a temporary, messy aberration in the eternal perfection of nothingness, and that its work was a form of mercy. It came to Thronegate not as an attacker but as a missionary, utterly convinced that Godric, as the most powerful being in existence, must surely also be the most burdened. It offered to unmake him first, to grant him the peace of non-being before it graciously extended the same gift to everything else.

Godric received the Unraveler in the Hall of Eons, a vast chamber built from crystallized time-strata that recorded the entire history of Thronegate in its glimmering walls. He listened to the Unraveler's sermon with polite, patient attention, nodding occasionally as the entity described the peace of non-existence, the quiet dignity of returning to the void. When the Unraveler finished speaking, Godric stroked his salt-and-pepper beard thoughtfully, exchanged a brief glance with me that was almost conspiratorially amused, and remarked that he had recently acquired a superpower called Absolute Annihilation. He was not entirely certain what it did in its current, evolved state—the Weakness Paradox Evolution had added so many enhanced effects over the millennia that he had lost track—but he was curious to see how it functioned. He asked the Unraveler if it would consent to a demonstration.

The Unraveler, still smiling with the serene condescension of a being who believed itself beyond all harm, agreed. It had survived the unmaking of multiverses. It had witnessed the death of concepts and the dissolution of fundamental laws. It believed there was no force in existence that could threaten its nature as a living negation. It was, in the most literal sense, already nothing. What could annihilation possibly do to something that was already the absence of existence?

I need you to understand what I witnessed. I need you to understand that the base effect of Absolute Annihilation, nerfed to zero percent of its power, is sufficient to destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over. This is not hyperbole. This is not the frightened exaggeration of a traumatized wizard. This is the most precise, clinical description I can offer. And Godric was not nerfing the power. He was simply using it, with that same casual, absent-minded curiosity he brings to testing a new blend of tea or examining an interesting geological specimen. He raised his index finger—a gesture so small and simple that it barely registered as movement—and he directed the faintest, most infinitesimally tiny pulse of Absolute Annihilation toward the Unraveler.

The Unraveler was not destroyed. Destruction is a process. It has stages. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. What happened to the Unraveler was not a process. It was a retroactive elimination of the very possibility of its existence. Absolute Annihilation did not attack the Unraveler's form, or its consciousness, or its ontological nature, or its conceptual foundation. It attacked the fundamental logical proposition that the Unraveler had ever been capable of existing in the first place. The enhanced effects, layered and compounded by millennia of Evolved Weakness Paradox triggers, had transformed the superpower from a simple destructive force into something far more horrifying. The first enhanced effect was Absolute Spatial Disregard—the power did not travel from Godric's finger to the Unraveler. It simply occupied every spatial coordinate the Unraveler had ever occupied, across every dimension and every timeline, simultaneously. The second enhanced effect was Absolute Temporal Cancellation—the power annihilated the Unraveler not just in the present moment but in every past moment, every future moment, every possible moment, erasing its existence from the complete timeline of reality. The third enhanced effect was Absolute Conceptual Obliteration—the power unmade not just the entity but the abstract concept of the entity, the idea of the Unraveler, the memory of its existence in every mind that had ever perceived it.

And then there was the fourth enhanced effect. The one that permanently, irrevocably broke something inside me.

Absolute Annihilation possessed a parasitic amplification function. When it annihilated a target, it consumed the totality of that target's existence—every scrap of power, every fragment of knowledge, every trace of ontological weight—and converted it into a permanent, passive increase to Godric's already impossible base power. The Unraveler, a being that had unmade multiverses, that had existed since before the first universe, that possessed power beyond the comprehension of most cosmic hierarchies, was absorbed entirely. And the increase to Godric's power was a number so infinitesimally small, so close to absolute zero, that I cannot express it. The Unraveler was a single drop of water, and Godric was an infinite ocean. The drop was added, and the ocean did not measurably change. The gap between the Unraveler's power and Godric's power was so vast that consuming the Unraveler's existence increased Godric's strength by an amount that was, for all practical purposes, nothing. And yet, if that same power had been granted to any other entity in the omniverse, it would have been the most staggering, incomprehensible, reality-shattering amplification in the history of creation. To Godric, it was less than rounding error. Less than the heat-death of a single atom in the farthest corner of a forgotten dimension.

The collateral damage was beyond omniversal annihilation. Absolute Annihilation, once released, did not stop. The power, even restricted to an infinitesimal pulse, possessed a recursive chain-reaction function that propagated across all layers of reality. It annihilated the omniverse. Then it annihilated the void left behind by the omniverse. Then it annihilated the conceptual framework that had once contained the void. Then it annihilated the annihilation of that framework. Layer after layer, reality and non-reality and the spaces between them were consumed in a cascading, accelerating, infinitely recursive erasure that continued long after there was nothing left to erase. I existed in a state of total, absolute non-existence, preserved by Godric's protective auras, and I could feel the annihilation still propagating, still hungry, still hunting for any fragment of existence that might have escaped its initial sweep. It would have continued forever if Godric had not, with a casual thought, recalled the power and restored the omniverse in zero seconds.

The Unraveler was gone. Not dead. Not destroyed. Gone in a way that made the very concept of "gone" feel inadequate. Its existence had been canceled. Its memory had been erased from every mind except those Godric chose to preserve. Its name, its purpose, its history, its impact on the multiverses it had unmade—all of it was retroactively removed from the structure of reality. The omniverse was restored, but the Unraveler-shaped hole in existence remained, a permanent absence where something had once been, a silent testament to a superpower that had unmade the unmaker with the casualness of a man brushing dust from his sleeve.

Godric lowered his finger. He looked at the space where the Unraveler had stood, and he nodded slowly, a slight, thoughtful frown creasing his ruggedly handsome features. "Potent," he remarked to me, his rich baritone carrying the same tone he might use to comment on the strength of a particularly good wine. "Rather more potent than I anticipated. The enhanced effects are quite something, aren't they? I will have to be more careful with that one."

He turned to me, his salt-and-pepper beard framing a warm, apologetic smile. "Valen, my boy, you are trembling. Did the cold from the hall get to you? Let us retire to the library. There is a fire in the hearth, and I have been meaning to show you a new volume of poetry I acquired from the outer provinces. It is really quite lovely."

I followed him to the library. I sat by the fire. I listened to him read poetry while the omniverse outside the palace walls continued to exist in blissful ignorance of the fact that, for one endless, eternal moment, it had been annihilated by a superpower whose base effect, nerfed to zero percent, was sufficient to destroy everything infinite times over. The words of the poem were beautiful. I remember none of them. I remember only the sound of my own sanity creaking under the weight of what I had witnessed, and the terrifying, undeniable truth that Godric genuinely believed he had been restrained. He had used effectively zero percent of Absolute Annihilation's power, and the result had been a recursive cascade of erasure that unmade the omniverse, the void, the concept of the void, and the concept of concepts. He thought I was trembling from the cold.

I am trembling as I write this. It has been three days. The trembling has not stopped. I do not expect it will ever stop. I am loyal. I am his court wizard. I love him as my ancestor Blake loved him. But I have looked into the face of a power that could retroactively unmake existence itself, and I have seen that power wielded with the casual, absent-minded curiosity of a man testing a new kitchen knife. The stat journals warned me that Godric's attributes were beyond measurement. They did not warn me that his superpowers were beyond sanity.

If you are reading this, future court wizard, I am sorry. I am so deeply, profoundly sorry. And I beg you to understand: Absolute Annihilation is one superpower. He has gained one new superpower every second for over six hundred thousand years. The number of powers he possesses is itself beyond infinite comprehension. This was only one. There are countless more. And each one, in its current evolved state, is more horrifying than the last.

Pray you never witness one being tested.

---

Kaedan closed Valen's journal. His hands were steady, but that steadiness was the product of absolute concentration, a forced calm that he wrapped around himself like a shield. The stat journals had described annihilation on a scale that shattered reason. Valen's journal described annihilation that broke the concept of scale itself. The Unraveler had been unmade retroactively, its existence canceled from every timeline, its memory erased from every mind, its power consumed and added to Godric's infinite ocean without producing a measurable ripple. And Godric had nodded thoughtfully, called the power "potent," and invited his court wizard to listen to poetry by the fire.

The fifth enhanced effect, which Valen had described in a trembling addendum below his signature, was the one that lodged itself most deeply in Kaedan's mind. Absolute Annihilation, Valen wrote, possessed a passive, permanent function that required no activation. Any being, anywhere in the omniverse, who formulated the genuine intent to harm Godric would trigger a microscopic, automatic pulse of the superpower. The pulse would annihilate the hostile being so completely that their existence would be retroactively erased from the timeline, preventing the hostile intent from ever having formed in the first place. The being would never have existed. The threat would never have been conceived. The omniverse would simply continue, blissfully unaware that anything had ever been wrong. Godric had not mentioned this enhanced effect. Valen had deduced it from studying the residual energy patterns in the Hall of Eons after the demonstration. Godric's every superpower, he wrote, was not merely a weapon. It was a passive, autonomous defense system that operated entirely without his conscious involvement. He did not need to protect himself. His powers protected him, retroactively and automatically, and they had been doing so for hundreds of thousands of years without him ever giving the matter a moment's thought.

Kaedan reached for the second volume.

---

**Year 741,209 — Orin — Superpower: Absolute Transmutation**

I am Orin. I am forty-eight years old. I have served as Godric's court wizard for twenty-eight years, and I have just witnessed something that has permanently altered my understanding of the relationship between matter, energy, and the fundamental laws that govern their interaction. I am a scholar of transfiguration magic. I have spent my entire adult life studying the delicate art of transmutation—the careful, precise manipulation of physical substance from one form to another. I believed I understood the limits of what transmutation could achieve. I was, in the most absolute and humiliating sense, completely wrong.

The entity that came to Thronegate was called the Convalescent Host. It was not a single being but a collective intelligence composed of trillions of individual organisms, each one a master of adaptive biology and evolutionary transcendence. The Host had survived the destruction of its home multiverse by evolving beyond the need for physical form, then beyond the need for dimensional space, then beyond the need for temporal continuity. By the time it reached Thronegate, the Host had transformed itself into a distributed consciousness that existed across every possible state of matter, energy, and probability simultaneously. It could not be killed because killing required a target, and the Host was not a thing that could be targeted. It was a pattern, an arrangement, a probability distribution that could reform itself from any damage by simply rearranging its constituent parts. It had absorbed the collective knowledge of a billion civilizations. It had evolved beyond every weapon ever conceived. It believed itself to be the ultimate expression of adaptive existence, a being that could transmute itself to survive any threat, overcome any obstacle, outlast any enemy. It challenged Godric not with violence but with a philosophical proposition: that adaptation was the supreme law of reality, that anything that could not change would eventually be surpassed by something that could, and that Godric's static, unchanging nature was therefore his fundamental, inevitable weakness.

Godric received the Convalescent Host's philosophical manifesto with a warm, patient smile. He was seated in the palace's Hall of Reflection, a chamber designed for quiet contemplation, its walls covered in ancient tapestries depicting the early years of his reign. He read through the Host's arguments with the careful attention of a scholar reviewing a promising but flawed thesis, occasionally nodding at particularly eloquent passages. When he finished, he set the manifesto aside, stroked his salt-and-pepper beard with thoughtful deliberation, and remarked that he had a superpower called Absolute Transmutation. He had never tested it in its fully evolved state, he said, and he was curious to see what the enhanced effects could do. He asked the Host if it would be willing to participate in a small demonstration.

The Convalescent Host, supremely confident in its adaptive superiority, agreed. It had evolved beyond physical harm. It had evolved beyond conceptual attack. It had evolved beyond every category of threat the omniverse could produce. It believed there was no transmutation it could not adapt to, no transformation it could not survive and ultimately transcend. It prepared itself, spreading its consciousness across every possible state of existence, ready to adapt to whatever Godric's power might do.

Godric did not raise a hand. He did not gesture. He simply looked at the space where the Convalescent Host's primary manifestation hovered, and he allowed the most infinitesimally small fraction of Absolute Transmutation's power to express itself. The amount of power he used was effectively zero percent. It was an amount so small that calling it zero was the most accurate description language could provide.

Absolute Transmutation, in its evolved state, possessed multiple enhanced effects that had been layered onto the base power by repeated Evolved Weakness Paradox triggers over hundreds of thousands of years. The base effect alone—the ability to transform any form of matter, energy, or conceptual substance into any other form with absolute precision and zero energy loss—was already sufficient to destroy the omniverse infinite times over. The enhanced effects were obscenities stacked on top of obscenities. The first enhanced effect was Absolute Resistance Bypass. No form of adaptive immunity, no evolved defense, no conceptual shield could block or mitigate the transmutation. The power simply ignored resistance as though resistance did not exist, because, from the power's perspective, resistance was merely another form of matter to be transmuted. The second enhanced effect was Absolute Conservation Violation. The power could transform any target without regard for the conservation of mass, energy, or any other fundamental law. It could turn a single atom into a galaxy, or a galaxy into a single atom, and the balance of the universe would simply adjust to accommodate the change. The third enhanced effect was Absolute Anatomical Comprehension. The power knew, with perfect, infinite precision, the exact physical, conceptual, and existential structure of any target, and could alter that structure at any level, from the sub-quantum to the meta-dimensional, with surgical precision.

The fourth enhanced effect was the one that will haunt me until I die. Absolute Transmutation possessed what I can only describe as a creative sadism function. The power did not simply transform the target into a new form. It calculated, with infinite precision, the form that would cause the target the maximum possible amount of suffering while still preserving the target's consciousness to experience that suffering. It then executed that transformation with the efficiency of a master surgeon and the cruelty of an eternal tormentor.

The Convalescent Host was not destroyed. It was remade. Its trillions of individual organisms, distributed across every state of matter, energy, and probability, were simultaneously and instantaneously transmuted into a single, unified physical form. Absolute Resistance Bypass ignored every adaptive defense the Host had evolved. Absolute Conservation Violation compressed the Host's distributed consciousness into a physical volume no larger than a human torso. Absolute Anatomical Comprehension mapped every nerve, every synapse, every possible pain receptor, and calculated the precise arrangement that would maximize agony. The creative sadism function then executed its design.

The Host became a single, solid block of living, screaming nerve tissue. Every square millimeter of its new form was hyper-dense with pain receptors, each one permanently activated at maximum intensity, each one connected to a consciousness that the transmutation had deliberately preserved and amplified to ensure it could experience every microsecond of its torment with perfect, undiluted clarity. The Host was not dying. The transmutation had rendered it biologically immortal, its cellular structure locked into a state of permanent, unchanging agony. It would never decay. It would never die. It would never adapt to the pain, because the pain was not an external stimulus; it was now the fundamental state of its existence, woven into the very fabric of its being.

And then there was the fifth enhanced effect. The parasitic amplification function. When Absolute Transmutation transformed a target, it permanently absorbed the target's most valuable attributes and integrated them into Godric's base power. The Convalescent Host's billion-year legacy of adaptive evolution, its trillions of organisms' collective knowledge, its mastery of biological transcendence—all of it was consumed and added to Godric's infinite reservoir of power. And the increase was, as always, functionally zero. The Host's accumulated power, which would have elevated any other being in the omniverse to godhood beyond godhood, was a single grain of sand added to an infinite desert. The gap did not change. The gap could not change. The ocean of Godric's power was already so vast that adding the entire omniverse to it would produce an increase that was, for all practical purposes, nothing.

The collateral damage from the demonstration was catastrophic beyond my ability to properly describe. Absolute Transmutation, once unleashed, did not limit itself to the intended target. The power's passive area of effect extended across every dimension, every timeline, every level of reality. Everything that existed, across the entire omniverse, was simultaneously and instantaneously transmuted into a random state before being transmuted back in the same infinitesimal moment. For one timeless instant, every star in every universe became a block of screaming nerve tissue, every planet became a sphere of liquid gold, every living being became a perfect crystal statue of themselves. Then, in the next instant, everything was restored. The omniverse did not even register the change. Only I, preserved and protected by Godric's auras, witnessed the flicker of absolute, omniversal transformation. Only I saw the entire creation blink into a state of cosmic horror and back again in less than a single Planck second.

Godric observed the screaming, immortal block of nerve tissue that had once been the Convalescent Host with a slight, thoughtful tilt of his head. The block was emitting a continuous, high-pitched whine—the sound of trillions of consciousnesses trapped in a state of perfect, undiluted agony, unable to move, unable to die, unable to do anything except scream. Godric studied it for a long moment, then nodded slowly.

"Remarkable," he said softly. "The enhanced effects are extremely thorough. I had not anticipated the creative sadism function. How fascinating that the power develops its own aesthetic preferences."

He turned to me, his salt-and-pepper beard framing a warm, slightly concerned smile. "Orin, my friend, you look as though you have seen a ghost. Let us leave this place. The air in here has become rather oppressive. I know a quiet spot in the eastern gardens where the jasmine is in bloom. We can discuss your latest research on transfiguration theory. I have been meaning to ask you about the third chapter of your treatise—the section on elemental conversion efficiency is particularly promising."

I followed him to the gardens. I sat among the blooming jasmine. I discussed elemental conversion efficiency with a being who had just demonstrated a superpower that could transmute the entire omniverse into a monument of screaming agony with less than a thought. He was genuinely interested in my research. He asked insightful questions. He offered suggestions that, I realized with a cold, hollow feeling, were technically perfect—because his absolute omniscience already knew the answers to the questions I was spending my life trying to solve. He was humoring me. Kindly, warmly, genuinely humoring me, because he loved me as he loved all of Blake's descendants, and he wanted me to feel useful and valued.

The Convalescent Host remains in the Hall of Reflection, encased in a stasis field that Godric erected to prevent its screams from disturbing the palace staff. He visits it occasionally, not out of cruelty but out of scientific curiosity. He is studying the creative sadism function. He wants to understand how his power decides what constitutes maximum suffering. He has taken notes. He has asked me to review his observations. I have done so, because I am his loyal court wizard, and because the alternative—refusing him, disappointing him—is something I cannot bring myself to contemplate.

I am Orin. I am forty-eight years old. I will serve Godric until I die. And I will never, for a single moment of my remaining years, forget the sound that the Convalescent Host makes when it screams. It is a sound that has no comparison. It is the sound of a superpower's idea of artistic expression.

---

Kaedan set Orin's journal down with hands that were no longer steady. The forced calm he had maintained after reading Valen's account had cracked, and through the cracks, something cold and dark was seeping into his consciousness. The Convalescent Host had been a being of supreme adaptive evolution, a distributed intelligence that had survived the destruction of its home multiverse. And Godric had transformed it into a block of screaming nerve tissue as a casual demonstration, then studied the result with scientific curiosity, then asked his court wizard for feedback on his observational notes. The creative sadism function, Orin had written in a cramped, barely legible postscript, was not unique to Absolute Transmutation. Every superpower in Godric's arsenal had evolved similar functions, customized to the power's specific nature. The Evolved Weakness Paradox had not merely made each superpower stronger; it had made each superpower cruel, creative, and terrifyingly autonomous. The powers had preferences. The powers had aesthetics. The powers were, in some sense, alive, and they loved their master with a devotion that expressed itself through the imaginative destruction of anything that opposed him.

Kaedan's father had told him that the superpower journals were different from the stat journals. He was beginning to understand. The stat journals described measurement. The superpower journals described artistry. Godric's powers were not content to simply destroy; they sought to express themselves, to create experiences of suffering and annihilation that were unique, memorable, almost beautiful in their horrific precision. And Godric, the warm, kind, genuinely loving King of Thronegate, observed these expressions with the detached curiosity of an art critic evaluating a new painting.

He reached for the third volume.

---

**Year 889,556 — Felix — Superpower: Absolute Projection**

My name is Felix. I am fifty-four years old, and I have served as Godric's court wizard for thirty-four years. I am writing this account with a clarity that I suspect will be mistaken for calm. It is not calm. It is the stillness of a mind that has been pushed so far beyond its capacity for fear that fear itself has become meaningless. I have witnessed Godric test his strength before. I have witnessed him test his endurance. I have read the journals of my predecessors and absorbed their warnings with the solemn attention they deserve. Nothing—nothing in those journals, nothing in my own experience, nothing in the collected wisdom of our bloodline—could have prepared me for what I witnessed today.

The opposition was not a single entity or a unified civilization. It was a universal-scale military coalition composed of the surviving remnants of every force that had ever opposed Godric and been defeated. The shattered kingdoms, the broken empires, the scattered survivors of a hundred thousand failed resistances had gathered in the void between universes, pooling their residual power, their forbidden weapons, their desperate, hopeless strategies into one final, sacrificial assault. They called themselves the Last Vengeance. They numbered in the septillions. Their armada stretched across a volume of space larger than a googolplexian galaxy clusters. They had weapons forged from the corpses of dead gods, armor woven from the conceptual fabric of defiance, and a collective hatred so pure and so absolute that it had crystallized into a tangible force that distorted the fabric of reality around their fleet. They knew they would lose. They had absorbed the lessons of every failed opposition that had come before them. They understood that Godric's stats were beyond measurement, that his passive defenses were insurmountable, that the Weakness Paradox would turn every strategy against them. They attacked anyway, not because they believed they could win, but because they believed that defiance itself was a moral imperative. They wanted to make a statement. They wanted to show the omniverse that even absolute futility could not extinguish the will to resist.

Godric received the reports of the Last Vengeance's approach with the same warm, slightly distracted attention he gave to all such news. He was in the throne room, reviewing a petition from one of the outer provinces regarding water rights—a matter he found genuinely interesting, as it reminded him of the irrigation disputes he had mediated during his mortal reign over Olympus. He listened to my tactical briefing, nodded along politely, and then remarked that he had been curious about a superpower called Absolute Projection. He was not entirely certain what it did in its current, fully evolved state, but the name suggested something to do with force projection, and the Last Vengeance seemed like a suitable opportunity for a field test. He asked me if I would like to accompany him to the observation deck.

The observation deck of the Eternal Palace is a platform of polished obsidian that floats high above the capital city, offering an unobstructed view of the surrounding space. The Last Vengeance fleet was visible as a vast, glittering smear of light on the horizon, a galaxy-spanning armada of desperate, doomed warriors. Godric stood at the edge of the platform, his ruggedly handsome face lit by the distant glow of their engines, his salt-and-pepper hair ruffled by the artificial breeze of the palace's environmental systems. He looked at the armada for a long moment, then nodded to himself. He raised his hand, palm outward, fingers slightly curled, in a gesture that was almost casual—the sort of gesture a man might make to signal a friend across a crowded room. He activated Absolute Projection at effectively zero percent of its power.

The base effect of Absolute Projection, I would later learn, is the ability to manifest autonomous, sentient extensions of Godric's power at any location in the omniverse. These projections are not illusions or copies; they are discrete, self-aware entities, each one possessing an infinitesimal fraction of Godric's total might. But that infinitesimal fraction, like every infinitesimal fraction of Godric's power, is more than sufficient to destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over. The projections are not weakened versions of Godric; they are concentrated expressions of specific aspects of his power, given form, given consciousness, given the absolute loyalty and protective ferocity that characterizes all of his autonomous systems.

The enhanced effects, layered by repeated Evolved Weakness Paradox triggers over nearly nine hundred thousand years, had transformed Absolute Projection into something far beyond simple force projection. The first enhanced effect was Infinite Projective Multiplication. Godric did not need to consciously determine the number of projections to create; the power itself analyzed the size, composition, and threat level of the opposition and generated the precise number of projections required to achieve total annihilation with maximum efficiency. The second enhanced effect was Absolute Projective Autonomy. Each projection was a fully independent entity with its own tactical intelligence, its own creative problem-solving abilities, its own capacity for strategic adaptation. They did not need to be commanded or directed. They knew, with the perfect clarity of their divine origin, exactly what their master wanted. The third enhanced effect was Projective Omniversal Reach. The projections could manifest anywhere, at any time, in any dimension, across any timeline, without regard for distance, barriers, or temporal constraints. The fourth enhanced effect was Projective Adaptive Escalation. Each projection, upon encountering resistance, would instantly amplify its own power to precisely the level needed to overwhelm that resistance with a margin of at least one hundred times the opposition's maximum capacity. And if the opposition somehow survived that escalation, the projection would escalate again, and again, without limit, until the resistance was annihilated.

The fifth enhanced effect was the one that stilled my heart in my chest. Absolute Projection possessed what I can only describe as Projective Aesthetic Coordination. The projections did not simply destroy the opposition; they coordinated their destruction to create patterns, formations, visual displays of annihilation that were, in their horrifying way, beautiful. They turned the act of omnicide into an art form. Each projection moved in perfect synchronization with every other projection, their attacks weaving together into a tapestry of destruction that was simultaneously the most efficient possible method of annihilation and the most visually spectacular display of overwhelming force I have ever witnessed.

Godric activated the power. And the omniverse filled with God.

The projections manifested simultaneously across the entire volume of the Last Vengeance fleet. There were exactly as many projections as there were enemy combatants—not one more, not one less. Infinite Projective Multiplication had calculated the optimal deployment with perfect precision. Each projection appeared directly in front of a single enemy soldier, a single warship, a single god-weapon. Each projection was simultaneously unique—shaped by Absolute Projective Autonomy to match the specific fears, the specific nightmares, the specific psychological vulnerabilities of its assigned target—and unified by Projective Aesthetic Coordination into a single, vast, omniversal-scale display of absolute supremacy.

The projections did not use weapons. They did not need weapons. Each one was a concentrated fragment of Godric's power, and that power, even reduced to a fraction so infinitesimal it lacked any mathematical description, was more than the entire Last Vengeance fleet combined, multiplied by itself an infinite number of times. The projections simply advanced. They walked through the armada like a man walking through morning mist, and the armada dissolved around them. Warships larger than solar systems crumpled into neutronium spheres the size of marbles. God-weapons forged from the hatred of pantheons shattered into clouds of inert dust. Warriors who had trained for millennia in forbidden combat arts were reduced to expanding clouds of superheated plasma by the mere proximity of the projections' presence. The septillions of soldiers in the Last Vengeance fleet experienced, each one of them, a personalized death. The projections, guided by Projective Adaptive Escalation, calibrated their attacks to overcome each target's specific defenses, immunities, and survival mechanisms with exactly one hundred times the necessary force. No target required a second strike. No target had time to scream. The entire armada, a fleet larger than a googolplexian galaxy clusters, was annihilated so quickly that the light from their destruction had not yet reached the observation deck by the time the last soldier died. We watched the annihilation in silence, the distant fleet flickering and dying like a candle flame in a hurricane. Then Projective Aesthetic Coordination executed its final gesture. The expanding clouds of plasma, dust, and residual energy left behind by the destroyed fleet were shaped, by the coordinated movements of the projections, into a single, vast image visible across the entire local multiverse: the royal seal of Thronegate, rendered in the death-glow of a septillion extinguished lives, a monument of annihilation that would linger in the void for the next ten thousand years as a silent warning to any who might consider following the Last Vengeance's example.

The projections, their work complete, did not fade or dissolve. They turned in unison toward the Eternal Palace and knelt. Septillions of autonomous, sentient fragments of Godric's power, each one individually capable of annihilating the omniverse infinite times over, knelt in the void of space, facing their master. The image of a septillion divine entities bowing in perfect synchronization is burned into my memory with the permanence of a brand. Then, with a single, unified pulse of acknowledgment, they vanished, their purpose fulfilled, their existence no longer required.

And then there was the sixth enhanced effect. The one that Felix, in his trembling postscript, had underlined three times.

Absolute Projection possessed a passive, permanent function called Projective Absorption. When the projections destroyed a target, they absorbed every fragment of the target's knowledge, power, experience, and existential weight and transmitted it back to Godric. The Last Vengeance, a coalition composed of the remnants of every force that had ever opposed Godric, represented a collective reservoir of knowledge and power that stretched back across nearly a million years of failed resistances. All of it was absorbed. All of it was added to Godric's base power. And the increase was, as it had always been, as it would always be, functionally zero. The accumulated power of every enemy Godric had ever faced, concentrated into a single, unified transfer, added to his infinite ocean without producing a measurable ripple. The drop had become a slightly larger drop. The ocean remained unchanged.

Godric lowered his hand. He observed the fading image of the Thronegate royal seal, painted in the death-glow of a septillion extinguished lives across the face of the void, and he smiled. It was not a cruel smile. It was the smile of an artist who had just watched a particularly satisfying performance, or a craftsman admiring a well-made piece of furniture.

"That was unexpectedly elegant," he said, his rich baritone carrying a note of genuine, warm appreciation. "Projective Aesthetic Coordination is a delightful touch. I had not realized the power had developed such a refined creative sensibility."

He turned to me, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the distant, fading glow of the monument his projections had created. "Felix, my old friend, you look as though you could use a strong drink. Let us repair to my study. I have a bottle of aged brandy that I have been saving for a special occasion, and I believe witnessing the debut of Absolute Projection's enhanced effects qualifies. Come, come. You must tell me honestly what you thought of the final display. I am not certain the royal seal was the most inspired choice, but the projections seemed quite committed to it."

I followed him. I drank his brandy. It was excellent, aged for forty thousand years in casks carved from the heartwood of a now-extinct dimensional tree. I told him honestly that the final display was, in my opinion, the most terrifyingly beautiful thing I had ever witnessed. He thanked me for my candor and poured me another glass.

The Last Vengeance is gone. The monument of their destruction still floats in the void, visible from a trillion light-years in every direction, a permanent reminder of what happens when Absolute Projection is tested at effectively zero percent of its power. I am Felix. I am fifty-four years old. I will serve Godric until I die. And I have made peace with the knowledge that my king is not merely the most powerful being in existence; he is the source of powers that are themselves artists of annihilation, and he admires their work with the same gentle, paternal pride he shows toward his court wizards. The horror of this cannot be overstated. But neither can the love.

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. The Scriptorium was silent. The arcane lamps flickered softly, casting long shadows across the obsidian table and the scattered volumes that surrounded him. He sat without moving for a long time, his hands resting flat on the leather cover of Felix's journal, his breath slow and measured, his mind struggling to process the cumulative weight of everything he had read.

Eight journals. Five stat incidents. Three superpower demonstrations. In every case, Godric had exerted effectively zero percent of his true power. In every case, the opposition had been annihilated with such absolute, effortless finality that the very concept of resistance had been rendered absurd. In every case, Godric had then expressed warm, genuine, paternal concern for the wellbeing of the traumatized wizard standing beside him.

But the superpower journals were different. Kaedan could feel the difference in his bones. The stat journals described power beyond measurement; the superpower journals described power beyond sanity. Absolute Annihilation, which could retroactively erase an entity from every timeline and convert its existence into a permanent, passive power increase so infinitesimally small that it did not register. Absolute Transmutation, which could transform a distributed adaptive intelligence into a screaming block of immortal nerve tissue and then study the result with scientific curiosity. Absolute Projection, which could generate septillions of autonomous divine fragments that coordinated their annihilation into a visually spectacular monument of omnicide and then knelt in unison before their master. And these were only three superpowers. Godric had gained one new superpower every second for nearly nine hundred thousand years. The number of powers he possessed was, as Valen had written, beyond infinite comprehension.

The door opened. Aldric stepped inside, his ancient eyes immediately finding his son's face. He did not speak. He simply looked at the stack of journals, then at Kaedan's still, pale features, and he nodded slowly.

"You understand now," Aldric said quietly. It was not a question.
